content: Radiant Capital (Oct 2024, ~$50M): Attackers compromised multiple developers' hardware wallets through sophisticated malware, signing malicious multisig transactions. Root cause: insufficient operational security for multisig signers.

Munchables (Mar 2024, ~$62M): A rogue developer inserted a backdoor into an upgradeable proxy contract during deployment, assigning themselves a massive ETH balance in an uninitialized storage slot. Root cause: insufficient code review of deployment scripts.
PlayDapp (Feb 2024, ~$290M): Attacker gained access to deployer private key and minted 1.79 billion PLA tokens. Root cause: privileged minting role with no cap and no timelock/multisig protection.

Sonne Finance (May 2024, ~$20M): Classic empty market donation attack on Compound v2 fork. Attacker front-ran first deposit in new market to inflate exchange rate. Root cause: no minimum deposit protection during market initialization.

Penpie (Sep 2024, ~$27M): Reentrancy in Pendle market staking via malicious SY token that re-entered during reward distribution. Root cause: allowed arbitrary market tokens without reentrancy guards on claim path.
Polter Finance (Nov 2024, ~$12M): Flash loan oracle manipulation on Fantom via SpookySwap LP token price. Root cause: spot LP pricing without TWAP or manipulation resistance.
Bybit (Feb 2025, ~$1.5B): Largest crypto theft in history. Attackers compromised MPC signing infrastructure for cold wallet. Root cause: operational security failure at signing infrastructure layer.

Abracadabra/MIM (Jan 2025, ~$13M): Liquidation logic flaw in Cauldron V4 on Arbitrum. Attacker manipulated borrow accounting during partial liquidations. Root cause: incorrect state update ordering in liquidation path.

Cross-function reentrancy via ERC-777/ERC-1155 callbacks: Even with per-function reentrancy guards, attackers can re-enter a different function that reads stale state. Root cause: function-level nonReentrant doesn't protect cross-function invariants. Use contract-level locks.

Read-only reentrancy in Balancer/Curve pools: Attacker triggers callback during pool operation, then calls view function (getRate, get_virtual_price) in another protocol reading intermediate state. Root cause: view functions not protected by reentrancy locks.

Restaking protocol risks (2024-2025): EigenLayer introduces novel vectors: cascading slashing across AVSs, operator collusion, withdrawal delay manipulation, shares accounting errors during slashing. Root cause: composability of staking positions creates correlated risk.

L2 sequencer downtime oracle risk: When L2 sequencer goes down, Chainlink oracles stop updating but stale prices remain readable. Protocols may allow trades at stale prices when sequencer restarts. Mitigation: integrate L2 Sequencer Uptime Feed with grace period.

Intent-based protocol front-running: In intent/solver architectures (UniswapX, CoW Swap), malicious solvers can sandwich user intents, selectively fill profitable ones, or manipulate price between submission and execution. Root cause: asymmetric information.

PUSH0 opcode incompatibility: Solidity >=0.8.20 uses PUSH0 (EIP-3855), unsupported on chains without Shanghai upgrade. Deploying on unsupported chains causes immediate revert. Root cause: compiler default EVM version mismatch.

Transient storage (EIP-1153) reentrancy guard considerations: TSTORE/TLOAD reset after each transaction. In delegate-call contexts, transient storage is scoped to calling contract not implementation, potentially breaking assumptions.

Price oracle manipulation via low-liquidity Uniswap V3 pools: TWAP on V3 pools can be manipulated cheaply if tick liquidity is concentrated in narrow ranges. Root cause: assuming TWAP is manipulation-resistant without verifying pool liquidity depth.

Unsafe int256/uint256 casting: Solidity 0.8.x prevents overflow on arithmetic but NOT on explicit type casts. Casting negative int256 to uint256 produces very large number. Root cause: explicit casts bypass overflow protection.

Return bomb attack: Malicious contract returns extremely large payload. If calling contract copies all returndata to memory, causes out-of-gas. Root cause: unbounded returndatasize copy. Mitigation: use assembly to limit or excessivelySafeCall pattern.

msg.value reuse in multicall/batch patterns: With delegatecall-based multicall, same msg.value is accessible in every sub-call. Attacker sending 1 ETH uses it across N operations. Affected Sushi RouteProcessor2 (~$3.3M). Root cause: msg.value not decremented in delegatecall.

ERC-2771 trusted forwarder + multicall vulnerability: Attacker appends victim address to calldata in multicall sub-call, spoofing _msgSender() when msg.sender is trusted forwarder. Patched in OpenZeppelin 4.9.4.

LRT (Liquid Restaking Token) de-peg risk: LRTs can de-peg from ETH during withdrawal delays or slashing. Protocols using LRT as collateral at 1:1 are exposed to bad debt. Root cause: assuming LRT == ETH without accounting for redemption delays.

Flash loan oracle manipulation via ERC-4626 share price: Attacker donates to vault to inflate convertToAssets() price, borrows against inflated collateral. Root cause: using manipulable on-chain share price as oracle without time-weighting.

Merkle tree proof vulnerabilities in airdrops: (1) Leaf pre-image collision without length-prefixing, (2) inner nodes as valid leaves, (3) missing claim tracking. Mitigation: use keccak256(abi.encode(address, amount)) with double-hashing.

Compound/Aave fork liquidation bonus miscalculation: Modified bonus without updating check that bonus doesn't exceed collateral value. Liquidators extract more collateral than debt, creating bad debt. Root cause: inconsistent parameter updates.

Decimal precision mismatch in cross-token calculations: Computing ratios between tokens with different decimals (USDC 6 vs ETH 18) without normalization leads to 10^12x over/under-valuation. Root cause: inconsistent decimal handling.

FP: Reentrancy flagged on view/pure functions calling trusted immutable contracts (e.g. Chainlink). Only exploitable if read value controls state changes AND called contract has mutable state during the call.
FP: Centralization risk flagged for onlyOwner behind 7-day timelock + 4/7 multisig. Not a meaningful risk for most threat models. Only flag if no timelock or single-key ownership.
FP: Unbounded loop gas DoS on admin-only arrays with practical size limits (10-50 items). Key check: who controls array length and what's realistic maximum?
FP: Front-running flagged on permissioned functions (onlyKeeper, onlyRelayer). If only whitelisted addresses can call, public mempool front-running is not a concern.
FP: Integer overflow in Solidity >=0.8.x where checks are built-in. Only flag arithmetic issues inside unchecked{} blocks or explicit type casts.
FP: Timestamp dependence for non-critical operations (logging, UI display, cache expiration). Only flag when timestamp controls financial outcomes or randomness.
FP: Low-level call without success check flagged when require(success) is present. Address.sendValue() from OpenZeppelin wraps this pattern safely.
FP: Floating pragma in library/interface files. Only flag in deployment-ready contracts, not abstract contracts or interfaces.
FP: Missing zero-address check on immutable constructor parameters verified off-chain in deployment scripts. Flag only if address is mutable or user-supplied at runtime.
Audit methodology: Invariant-first approach. Enumerate all state invariants, then systematically test code paths for violations. Found 40%+ of critical findings in 2024 Code4rena contests.

Audit methodology: Token integration checklist. Verify handling of: fee-on-transfer, rebasing, >18/<6 decimals, zero-amount revert, blocklists, no-bool-return, permit, upgradeable proxies. Missed in 60%+ of DeFi audits.
Audit methodology: Trace value flow end-to-end. For every value-moving function verify sum(inputs) == sum(outputs) + sum(fees) at every step. Catches majority of accounting bugs.
Audit methodology: Verify assumptions at trust boundaries (user->contract, contract->oracle, L1->L2). 70%+ of critical vulnerabilities exist at trust boundaries.

Audit finding frequency (2024 contests): Missing validation ~18%, Incorrect accounting ~15%, Oracle manipulation ~12%, Access control ~10%, Reentrancy ~8%, Token integration ~8%, Precision ~7%, MEV ~6%, DoS ~5%, Cross-contract ~4%.
Audit methodology: Fuzzing-guided manual review. Brief Echidna/Foundry invariant fuzz runs (10 min) before manual review increases finding yield by ~35%.

Vyper compiler reentrancy bug aftermath (2024): Contracts compiled with Vyper 0.2.15-0.3.0 using @nonreentrant may have non-functional reentrancy guards. Must check compiler version for all Vyper contracts.

Reentrancy in ERC-721/1155 safe transfer callbacks: safeTransferFrom/safeMint invoke onERC721Received callback enabling re-entry. Frequently missed because devs don't associate NFT transfers with reentrancy.

DoS via block gas limit in unbounded batch operations: Functions processing unbounded arrays can exceed block gas limit as protocol grows, permanently bricking the function. Mitigation: paginated processing.
